focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 7

The next morning, the email from Research Integrity arrived while Leela sat at her desk in Keating trying to coax one dead benchmark run into becoming a paragraph she could still someday call a dissertation. Outside the sixth-floor window, Lakebridge had gone the color of dishwater, the wet part of cold rather than snow, a steady damp that climbed into coat sleeves and stayed there. The message carried a cheerful blue flag beside it, bright enough to feel hand-placed and intimate in the worst way.

Subject: Required Attendance - Research Integrity Intake

Dear Ms. Krishnan,

Please report to Baines Hall, Room 414, at 2:30 p.m. today regarding a data-handling concern associated with privileged access credentials under your stewardship.

The message was signed by Mara Sloane, Associate Director, and copied an Information Security address that looked automated enough to absolve everybody in advance.

Leela read it twice, then a third time with her hand flat on the desk so the tendons in her wrist would stop twitching under the brace. The phrase privileged access credentials under your stewardship sat in her head like a stain. Nobody in Keating said things like stewardship unless they were already arranging blame into folders.

Across the cubicle wall Omar said, "You made the face."

He had a pharmacy bag open on his desk and was eating pistachios one by one, setting the empty shells in a neat line along the edge of a conference flyer. Even hungry, even frightened, he organized his mess before it existed. He leaned back enough to see her over the partition, his beard line still too careful for a man who had slept in the department three nights the week before.

"What face?"

"The one you make when the machine has decided it knows more than you do."

She turned...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...mire her honesty. Leela had handed them that version with one careful sentence.

"When the split is clean," June said, touching the laser dot to the bottom table, "the improvement is modest and local. Fine. Then the honest claim is local. The benchmark still tells you something useful. It just doesn't get to swagger."

A chair leg screeched at the back. One of the first-years slipped out with half a cardamom knot wrapped in a napkin, trying for stealth and failing.

June clicked back to the architecture slide. "Model stack is the same. Thread context. Author-link heuristics. Error slices by euphemism family. Deployment still cares about the same ugly behavior. The cleaner split just takes a bat to the pretty number."

Halfway through the next architecture point her voice caught. She cleared it and kept going. On the second catch, the postdoc with the baby-sticker bottle stood, crossed the aisle, and set the bottle beside June's paper cup without a word.

The whole room saw it. After that nobody interrupted. Mohit stayed still in his chair. Ben did not resume typing.

June stopped for exactly one beat, nodded once without looking up, unscrewed the stranger's bottle with her free hand, and drank. Then she braced her left hand on the lectern and kept explaining the architecture while Professor Adler wrote down the smaller claim as June's headline all along.

Leela did not write anything down after that. She watched June finish the seminar with one hand braced on the lectern, voice lowered to something almost private, and knew the room would remember her composure as if it had not been extracted from her. When the questions finally dissolved into corridor talk and paper plates, Leela went looking for a floor of the building where nobody would know her face for five minutes.
